title: Cholinergic Agonists (ناهضات الكولين)
summary: ناهضات الكولين تنقسم إلى مباشرة (تعمل على مستقبلات M و N مباشرة) وغير مباشرة (مثبطات الكولينستريز). المستقبلات الموسكارينية (M1-M3) تتحكم في أعضاء الجهاز الهضمي والقلب والعين والغدد. مثبطات الكولينستريز القابلة للعكس (Neostigmine, Pyridostigmine) تُعالج الوهن العضلي الشديد. مركبات الفوسفور العضوي (Organophosphates) مثبطات لا رجعة فيها تسبب تسمما حادًا يُعالج بـ Atropine وPralidoxime.

key_drugs.name: Acetylcholine (ACh)
key_drugs.note: غير انتقائي، يُحلَّل سريعًا، استخدام محدود

key_drugs.name: Bethanechol
key_drugs.note: انتقائي للمثانة والجهاز الهضمي، مقاوم للكولينستريز

key_drugs.name: Neostigmine
key_drugs.note: لا يعبر BBB، للوهن العضلي وانتهاء مفعول المرخيات

key_drugs.name: Pyridostigmine
key_drugs.note: طويل المفعول، الخط الأول للوهن العضلي

mnemonics.title: DUMBELS: أعراض تسمم الفوسفور العضوي الموسكارينية
mnemonics.body:
D = Defecation (إسهال)
U = Urination (تبول)
M = Miosis (تضييق الحدقة)
B = Bronchospasm + Bradycardia (تشنج شعبي + بطء قلب)
E = Emesis (قيء)
L = Lacrimation (دموع)
S = Salivation (لعاب)
هذه الأعراض الموسكارينية تُعالج بـ Atropine

mnemonics.title: MATH: أعراض الفوسفور العضوي النيكوتينية
mnemonics.body:
M = Muscle tremors/fasciculations (رعاش عضلي)
A = Adrenal hyperactivity (فرط نشاط الكظر)
T = Tachycardia (تسرع القلب)
H = Hypertension (ارتفاع ضغط الدم)
هذه أعراض النيكوتين لا تستجيب للأتروبين

mnemonics.title: Neostigmine vs Physostigmine: الحاجز الدماغي
mnemonics.body: Neo = 'نيو يبقى خارج': Neostigmine لا يعبر BBB، يعمل على الجهاز العضلي الهيكلي والهضمي فقط. Physo = 'فيزو يدخل الجو': Physostigmine يعبر BBB ويصل الدماغ لعلاج تسمم Atropine.

mnemonics.title: Bethanechol: كرة المثانة
mnemonics.body: تخيل كرة تدفع المثانة والأمعاء للأمام: Bethanechol يُحرك المثانة والجهاز الهضمي (انتقائي لـ M3)، مقاوم للكولينستريز، يُستخدم للاحتباس البولي وشلل الأمعاء.

mnemonics.title: علاج تسمم الفوسفور: PAD
mnemonics.body:
P = Pralidoxime (PAM): يُعيد تنشيط الكولينستريز (أسرع ما يمكن قبل التقادم)
A = Atropine (جرعات كبيرة): لحصار الآثار الموسكارينية
D = Diazepam: للتشنجات
بالترتيب: أولًا حافظ على التنفس، ثم PAD

flashcards.front: What are the two main types of cholinergic receptors?
flashcards.back: Muscarinic (M1-M3) and Nicotinic (Nn, Nm)

flashcards.front: Which receptor is responsible for bradycardia?
flashcards.back: M2 on the sinoatrial and atrial nodes

flashcards.front: Which receptor is responsible for gastric acid secretion?
flashcards.back: M1

flashcards.front: Which receptor is responsible for pupillary constriction (miosis)?
flashcards.back: M3 on the iris sphincter muscle

flashcards.front: What is the difference between direct and indirect cholinergic agonists?
flashcards.back: Direct agonists activate the receptors directly; indirect agonists inhibit cholinesterase, thereby increasing ACh

flashcards.front: Why is Bethanechol preferred for treating urinary retention?
flashcards.back: It is selective for M3 in the bladder, resistant to hydrolysis, and has no effect on the heart

flashcards.front: What is the use of Pilocarpine?
flashcards.back: Treatment of glaucoma (miosis and fluid drainage) and dry mouth (Sjogren)

flashcards.front: What is the difference between Neostigmine and Edrophonium?
flashcards.back: Edrophonium is very short acting and used for diagnosis; Neostigmine is long acting and used for treatment

flashcards.front: What is the mechanism of action of Donepezil in Alzheimer's disease?
flashcards.back: A reversible cholinesterase inhibitor that increases ACh in the brain to improve memory

flashcards.front: What is the mechanism of death in organophosphate poisoning?
flashcards.back: Respiratory failure: paralysis of respiratory muscles + depression of the respiratory center + excessive bronchial secretions

flashcards.front: What is the first step in treating organophosphate poisoning?
flashcards.back: Maintaining airway, breathing, and circulation (ABC)

flashcards.front: Why must Pralidoxime be given quickly in poisoning?
flashcards.back: Because organophosphates bind the enzyme permanently over time (aging), after which it can no longer be reactivated

flashcards.front: What are the general side effects of cholinergic agonists?
flashcards.back: Sweating, miosis, salivation, bradycardia, bronchospasm, diarrhea, urination

flashcards.front: Which receptor controls skeletal muscle contraction?
flashcards.back: Nm (nicotinic muscle) at the neuromuscular junction

flashcards.front: What is myasthenia gravis?
flashcards.back: An autoimmune disease: antibodies against Nm receptors causing fatigable muscle weakness

flashcards.front: Which drug is used as first-line therapy for myasthenia gravis?
flashcards.back: Pyridostigmine (a long-acting reversible ChE inhibitor)

flashcards.front: Why is ACh not given systemically as a drug?
flashcards.back: It is non-selective (M+N), rapidly hydrolyzed by cholinesterase, and has very poor oral absorption

flashcards.front: What is the effect of ChE inhibition on the heart?
flashcards.back: Bradycardia and slowing of atrioventricular conduction (via M2)

flashcards.front: What is the difference between direct and indirect cholinergic agonists?
flashcards.back: Direct agonists bind the ACh receptor directly (Bethanechol, Pilocarpine); indirect agonists inhibit cholinesterase, thereby raising ACh (Neostigmine, Physostigmine).

flashcards.front: What is the difference between true and pseudo cholinesterase?
flashcards.back: True (AChE): at nerve endings, specific for ACh, essential for life. Pseudo (plasma ChE): in plasma/liver, non-specific, breaks down succinylcholine.

flashcards.front: What is the use of Bethanechol?
flashcards.back: Non-obstructive urinary retention and postoperative bladder/bowel atony.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between Neostigmine and Physostigmine?
flashcards.back: Physostigmine crosses the BBB (useful centrally); Neostigmine does not cross (peripheral - myasthenia gravis).

flashcards.front: What is the use of Neostigmine?
flashcards.back: Myasthenia gravis and reversal of non-depolarizing neuromuscular blockade.

flashcards.front: What are the effects of organophosphates?
flashcards.back: Irreversible cholinesterase inhibition → cholinergic excess (SLUDGE); antidote: Atropine + Pralidoxime.

flashcards.front: What does SLUDGE stand for?
flashcards.back: Salivation, Lacrimation, Urination, Defecation, GI distress, Emesis - symptoms of muscarinic overstimulation.

mcqs.q: A post-operative patient has urinary retention and paralytic ileus. Which drug is most appropriate?
mcqs.explanation: Bethanechol is a direct-acting M3-selective agonist in the bladder and GI tract; it is resistant to cholinesterase hydrolysis.

mcqs.q: A 75-year-old patient presents with progressive cognitive decline and memory loss. Which drug is appropriate?
mcqs.explanation: Donepezil is a reversible cholinesterase inhibitor that increases ACh in the brain and is first-line treatment for Alzheimer's disease.

mcqs.q: A farmer presents with excessive salivation, sweating, bradycardia, miosis, and bronchospasm. What is the first-line treatment?
mcqs.explanation: Organophosphate poisoning is treated with Atropine (to block muscarinic effects) plus Pralidoxime (to reactivate cholinesterase).

mcqs.q: What are the muscarinic symptoms of organophosphate toxicity (DUMBELS)?
options: Defecation, urination, miosis, bronchospasm, emesis, lacrimation, salivation
mcqs.explanation: DUMBELS = Defecation, Urination, Miosis, Bronchospasm, Emesis, Lacrimation, Salivation.

mcqs.q: Which cholinesterase inhibitor crosses the BBB and is used to reverse atropine toxicity?
mcqs.explanation: Physostigmine is a tertiary amine that crosses the BBB and is used to treat central atropine toxicity.

mcqs.q: What is the only clinical use of Edrophonium?
mcqs.explanation: Edrophonium has a very short duration of action (~5 minutes) and is used only for the Tensilon diagnostic test.

mcqs.q: Which cholinergic receptor is responsible for bronchial smooth muscle constriction?
mcqs.explanation: M3 on smooth muscle causes bronchoconstriction, GI contraction, and bladder contraction.

mcqs.q: What is the mechanism of irreversible ChE inhibitors (organophosphates)?
options: They bind cholinesterase permanently, increasing ACh
mcqs.explanation: Organophosphates form a covalent bond at the acetyl site of AChE, permanently preventing ACh hydrolysis.

mcqs.q: In myasthenia gravis, what type of autoantibodies cause the disease?
options: Against Nm receptors at the neuromuscular junction
mcqs.explanation: Myasthenia gravis is an autoimmune disease: IgG antibodies target Nm receptors at the neuromuscular junction.

mcqs.q: Why is acetylcholine not given orally?
options: It is a quaternary amine with poor oral absorption and rapid hydrolysis
mcqs.explanation: ACh is a quaternary ammonium compound that is poorly absorbed from the GI tract and is rapidly hydrolyzed by cholinesterase.

mcqs.q: Physostigmine differs from Neostigmine in that it:
mcqs.explanation: Physostigmine is a tertiary amine that crosses the blood-brain barrier.

mcqs.q: The antidote for organophosphate poisoning is:
mcqs.explanation: Atropine blocks muscarinic symptoms; Pralidoxime reactivates the inhibited enzyme.
Pilocarpine is a muscarinic agonist that reduces intraocular pressure.
